lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: The clock struck nine when I did send the nurse;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: In half an hour she promised to return.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Perchance she cannot meet him: that’s not so.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, she is lame! love’s heralds should be thoughts,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which ten times faster glide than the sun’s beams,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Driving back shadows over louring hills:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Therefore do nimble-pinion’d doves draw love,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And therefore hath the wind-swift Cupid wings.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Now is the sun upon the highmost hill

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Of this day’s journey, and from nine till twelve

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is three long hours, yet she is not come.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Had she affections and warm youthful blood,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: She would be as swift in motion as a ball;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My words would bandy her to my sweet love,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And his to me:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But old folks, many feign as they were dead;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Unwieldy, slow, heavy and pale as lead.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O God, she comes!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Peter, stay at the gate.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Now, good sweet nurse—O Lord, why look’st thou sad?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Though news be sad, yet tell them merrily;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If good, thou shamest the music of sweet news

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: By playing it to me with so sour a face.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I am a-weary, give me leave awhile:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Fie, how my bones ache! what a jaunt have I had!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I would thou hadst my bones, and I thy news.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Nay, come, I pray thee, speak; good, good nurse, speak.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Jesu, what haste? can you not stay awhile?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Do you not see that I am out of breath?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: How art thou out of breath, when thou hast breath

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To say to me that thou art out of breath?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: The excuse that thou dost make in this delay

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is longer than the tale thou dost excuse.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is thy news good, or bad? answer to that;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Say either, and I’ll stay the circumstance:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Let me be satisfied, is’t good or bad?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Well, you have made a simple choice; you know not how to

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: choose a man: Romeo! no, not he; though his face be better

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: than any man’s, yet his leg excels all men’s; and for a

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: hand, and a foot, and a body, though they be not to

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: be talked on, yet they are past compare: he is not the

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: flower of courtesy, but, I’ll warrant him, as gentle as a lamb.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Go thy ways, wench; serve God. What, have you dined at home?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: No, no: but all this did I know before.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What says he of our marriage? what of that?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Lord, how my head aches! what a head have I!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: It beats as it would fall in twenty pieces.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: My back o’ t’other side—O, my back, my back!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Beshrew your heart for sending me about,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To catch my death with jaunting up and down!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I’ faith, I am sorry that thou art not well.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Sweet, sweet, sweet nurse, tell me, what says my love?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Your love says, like an honest gentleman, and a courteous, and a

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: kind, and a handsome, and, I warrant, a virtuous—Where is your mother?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where is my mother! why, she is within;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where should she be? How oddly thou repliest!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: “Your love says, like an honest gentleman,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where is your mother?”

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O God’s lady dear!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Are you so hot? marry, come up, I trow;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Is this the poultice for my aching bones?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Henceforward do your messages yourself.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Here’s such a coil! come, what says Romeo?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Have you got leave to go to shrift to-day?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I have.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Then hie you hence to Friar Laurence’ cell;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: There stays a husband to make you a wife:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Now comes the wanton blood up in your cheeks,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: They’ll be in scarlet straight at any news.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Hie you to church; I must another way,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To fetch a ladder, by the which your love

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Must climb a bird’s nest soon when it is dark:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I am the drudge and toil in your delight,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: But you shall bear the burden soon at night.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Go; I’ll to dinner; hie you to the cell.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Hie to high fortune! Honest nurse, farewell.

stage_directions.text: O honey nurse, what news? Hast thou met with him? Send thy man away.

glosses.definition: noon position — the sun at its highest
toss her like a tennis ball back to my beloved
slow; cumbersome — Juliet impatiently describes old age
leaden, bloodless — the color of age and death
leave off the detail; get to the point
laborer — the Nurse calls herself Juliet's servant

annotations.title: Juliet's impatience — comic timing as character
annotations.body: Juliet's twelve-line soliloquy waiting for the Nurse is one of Shakespeare's loveliest exercises in comic timing through verse. She counts the hours, imagines the Nurse moving 'Unwieldy, slow, heavy, and pale as lead,' invokes the nimble doves of Venus. The scene is funny because the tempo of her language ('perchance she cannot meet him; that's not so') outruns her own reasoning. Yet the comedy is foreboding: Juliet's speed is exactly what the Friar has just warned against, and her wish ('Love's heralds should be thoughts, / Which ten times faster glide than the sun's beams') is the dramatic pressure that makes events outrun wisdom.
sources: Russ McDonald, Shakespeare and the Arts of Language (2001)

trials.prompt: Why is Juliet so impatient in this scene?
options: The Nurse is late returning with news from Romeo about the wedding plan
She is angry with her father
